finding: Panel LP-4A shows 312A connected load on a 200A feeder. The load schedule on E-104 doesn't match the feeder sizing on the one-line diagram E-302.
howItGotThere: Load schedule was revised during DD to add server rack circuits. The one-line wasn't updated in the same revision cycle.
consequence: Contractor RFI on day one. Feeder upsize requires larger conduit, larger breaker, possible upstream gear change.

finding: Specification references NFPA 70E arc flash labeling requirements but no arc flash study is called for anywhere in the documents or scoped to a responsible party.
howItGotThere: Spec was built from a template. The study requirement got copied but the execution scope didn't.
consequence: Contractor either excludes the study entirely (owner pays later as change order) or prices it as an allowance at 3x actual cost.

finding: CRAC unit discharge at grid line C-4 conflicts with structural beam. OEM-required 36" maintenance clearance cannot be achieved.
howItGotThere: Structural grid was finalized before CRAC locations were coordinated. Mechanical drew around the beam without verifying clearance requirements.
consequence: Discovered during rough-in. Either beam is modified (structural change order) or unit is relocated (mechanical rework plus ductwork rerouting).

finding: Generator day tank is specified for 8-hour runtime. Project runtime requirement stated in the Basis of Design is 24 hours.
howItGotThere: BOD was updated late in DD. Plumbing spec was carried forward from an earlier version.
consequence: Discovered during commissioning. Tank replacement plus fuel line rerouting plus containment revision.

finding: Door hardware schedule omits access control interface. Division 28 specification references an access control system that isn't listed in the door schedule or keyed to specific openings.
howItGotThere: Hardware schedule was built by the architect. Access control scope was written by the security consultant. The two documents never got cross-walked.
consequence: Contractor prices access control as an allowance (always high). Owner pays the delta after award as a change order.

finding: Lighting control specification requires LLLC commissioning per IECC. No responsible party is identified in the documents.
howItGotThere: Energy code triggered the commissioning requirement. Spec author flagged it but didn't assign it.
consequence: Commissioning agent excludes it. Contractor excludes it. Owner discovers the gap after occupancy.

finding: Basis of Design calls for N+1 cooling redundancy. Equipment schedule shows a single chiller with no standby.
howItGotThere: BOD was written in SD. Equipment schedule was developed independently in DD without being reconciled back to the BOD.
consequence: Owner discovers the gap during commissioning or, worse, during the first equipment failure.

finding: Room finish schedule specifies VCT in server room 204. Floor plan notes epoxy. Reflected ceiling plan shows neither.
howItGotThere: Three different consultants touched the finish documents at different stages. No one reconciled the final set.
consequence: Contractor RFI delays flooring procurement. If epoxy, lead time is 6–8 weeks and requires substrate prep not in the bid.

finding: Specification assigns UPS installation to the electrical contractor but UPS procurement to the owner. No interface document defines the handoff point, connection requirements, or startup responsibilities.
howItGotThere: Owner-furnished equipment scope was added late. Division 01 wasn't updated to reflect the interface.
consequence: Startup delay when contractor and owner each assume the other is responsible for connections and commissioning.

finding: Supply ductwork at ceiling grid line 8 conflicts with the main electrical cable tray shown on E-501. Both are drawn at the same elevation with no resolution noted.
howItGotThere: Mechanical and electrical drawings were developed in parallel without a coordinated ceiling model or clash matrix.
consequence: Field RFI during rough-in. One trade reroutes. Rerouting adds labor and material not in the bid.

finding: Medical gas outlets are shown on the drawings but the specification contains no Section 22 63 00 — Medical Gas and Vacuum Piping Systems.
howItGotThere: Spec was assembled from a commercial template. Medical gas section was never added.
consequence: Contractor either excludes medical gas entirely or prices it as a T&M allowance. Neither outcome is acceptable for a healthcare project.

finding: Addendum 3 revised the emergency panel schedule. The one-line diagram was not revised in the same addendum. Contractors bid two different configurations depending on which document they prioritized.
howItGotThere: Addendum was issued under time pressure. Only the schedule was revised, not the one-line.
consequence: Post-award scope dispute. One configuration requires additional conduit and breaker capacity.

finding: Roof assembly R-value shown on the drawings does not meet the energy code requirement for the project's climate zone. The spec calls out a compliant assembly but the drawings show a different one.
howItGotThere: Drawings were updated for a value-engineering substitution. The energy compliance documentation wasn't revised.
consequence: Building department flags during plan review. Redesign, revised submittal, schedule delay.

finding: Door 214A is scheduled as a 90-minute fire-rated assembly. The hardware set assigned to it includes a closer and lockset that are not listed on the manufacturer's fire rating label.
howItGotThere: Hardware schedule was spec'd by a hardware consultant. Fire rating was assigned by the architect. The two were never reconciled.
consequence: AHJ rejects the assembly. Replacement hardware required. If door is already hung, labor cost doubles.

finding: BAS specification calls for integration with the building's existing Siemens system. No points list, protocol document, or integration specification is included in the bid package.
howItGotThere: Integration scope was noted as 'TBD by controls contractor' during DD. The TBD was never resolved.
consequence: Every controls contractor bids a different scope. Post-award, the gap becomes a change order when the actual integration work is scoped.

finding: Electrical load calculations use a 0.85 power factor for the IT load. Owner's equipment vendor confirms the actual power factor is 0.6. The difference affects generator sizing by approximately 18%.
howItGotThere: Power factor assumption was carried from a prior project. IT equipment was specified after the electrical design was substantially complete.
consequence: Generator is undersized. Either a larger generator is required (procurement lead time: 20–26 weeks) or load shedding is designed in post-award.

finding: Fire alarm drawings show initiating devices in the server hall but no notification appliances. NFPA 72 requires both in an occupied space. The notification appliance circuit is missing from the riser diagram.
howItGotThere: Fire alarm was drawn by a subcontractor to the electrical engineer. The coordination between initiating and notification device coverage was not checked.
consequence: AHJ rejects fire alarm submittal. Redesign and additional devices required. If walls are closed, add demolition.

finding: Mechanical drawings show a 24" diameter penetration through the shear wall at grid line D between levels 2 and 3. Structural drawings show no opening, no sleeve, and no reinforcing detail.
howItGotThere: Penetration was added in a late mechanical revision. Structural was not notified.
consequence: Contractor cores the wall without structural review. Shear wall is compromised. Structural engineer of record issues RFI response requiring remediation.

finding: Generator fuel system includes a day tank and belly tank. No secondary containment is specified. Local jurisdiction requires 110% containment for fuel systems over 55 gallons.
howItGotThere: Containment requirement was not identified in the specification. Plumbing engineer was not aware of the local amendment to the fire code.
consequence: Building department flags during permit review. Containment system added as change order after structural slab is poured.

finding: Main switchgear is specified as 3000A, 480/277V. The one-line diagram shows a 2000A main breaker feeding the switchgear from the utility transformer. The mismatch makes the switchgear configuration unbuildable as drawn.
howItGotThere: Switchgear specification was written by the electrical engineer. The one-line was drawn by a different person in the same office and never cross-checked.
consequence: Contractor submits RFI before pricing. If not caught before bid, multiple contractors price different configurations and post-award reconciliation becomes a change order.

finding: Division 01 includes a $50,000 allowance for 'owner-directed specialty finishes.' No definition of what constitutes a specialty finish, no list of rooms where it applies, and no process for how the allowance is drawn down.
howItGotThere: Allowance was added at the owner's request during DD. The definition was deferred and never documented.
consequence: Post-award disputes about what the allowance covers. Contractor draws down the allowance on items the owner expected to be part of base scope.

finding: Exhaust fan EF-7 is shown discharging through the roof at grid line H-12. Structural drawings show a roof beam at the same location. No alternative discharge location is shown.
howItGotThere: Mechanical roof penetration plan was not overlaid on the structural framing plan during coordination.
consequence: Contractor cannot install as shown. Fan location is moved in the field, requiring ductwork rerouting and a new roof penetration detail.

finding: Data center specification requires a dedicated technical ground system per BICSI 002. No specification section, no drawing details, and no scope assignment exists for the technical ground installation.
howItGotThere: BICSI requirement was noted in the basis of design but never translated into a specification section or drawing requirement.
consequence: Contractor excludes technical ground. Owner discovers gap during IT equipment commissioning. Retrofit of grounding system after ceiling is closed is significantly more expensive.

finding: Storefront specification calls for thermally broken aluminum framing. The elevation drawings show a frame profile that is only available in non-thermally-broken configurations from the specified manufacturer.
howItGotThere: Elevation drawings were developed from a prior project. The thermal break requirement was added to the spec during energy compliance review but the drawing profile was not updated.
consequence: Contractor submits substitution request. If denied, the specified profile is custom-fabricated at a significant premium.

finding: Basis of Design specifies variable primary chilled water flow. Piping drawings show constant flow headers with no bypass or differential pressure control valve.
howItGotThere: BOD was written by the mechanical engineer of record. Piping drawings were produced by a design-assist subcontractor who was not given the BOD.
consequence: System operates outside design parameters. Variable flow benefits are not achieved. Controls contractor discovers the conflict during startup.

finding: NFPA 70E requires arc flash hazard analysis for all equipment where work may be performed while energized. No arc flash study is included in the project scope or referenced in the specification.
howItGotThere: Arc flash requirement is often overlooked on projects where the electrical engineer focuses on design rather than operations compliance.
consequence: Owner is non-compliant from day one. Study must be commissioned post-occupancy, often at 2x the pre-construction cost.

finding: Backflow preventer for the fire suppression system is shown in the mechanical room at an elevation of 8'-6" AFF. Ceiling height in that room is 8'-0".
howItGotThere: Backflow preventer elevation was specified without checking the ceiling height in the mechanical room.
consequence: Contractor cannot install as shown. Unit must be relocated, requiring revised pipe routing and potentially a different room.

finding: Division 01 submittal procedures require a 15-day review period. The project schedule shows long-lead equipment submittals being submitted 10 days before required approval dates to maintain the procurement schedule.
howItGotThere: Submittal schedule was built by the contractor. Division 01 review period was not factored in when the procurement schedule was set.
consequence: Either the review period is compressed (increasing review errors) or equipment procurement is delayed, pushing the construction schedule.

finding: Mechanical equipment schedule does not include seismic certification or anchorage requirements for any equipment. Project is in Seismic Design Category D.
howItGotThere: Seismic requirements were included in the structural specification but not coordinated with the mechanical specification or equipment schedule.
consequence: Building department flags during plan review. Equipment re-specification or engineering calculations required post-bid.

finding: Electrical conduit plan shows 4" conduit bank running through the interstitial space above the server hall. Mechanical drawings show chilled water supply and return mains occupying the same corridor at the same elevation.
howItGotThere: Electrical and mechanical drawings were produced in isolation. No coordinated interstitial plan was ever developed.
consequence: Field conflict during rough-in. One trade reroutes. Rerouting adds 60–80 linear feet of conduit or piping and delays the rough-in inspection.

finding: Conference room 318 requires STC 50 per the acoustic consultant's report. The partition type shown on the floor plan achieves STC 42 per the manufacturer's data.
howItGotThere: Acoustic consultant issued a separate report. The architect did not update the partition types on the drawings to match the consultant's recommendations.
consequence: Wall is built to STC 42. Owner measures performance after occupancy and requires remediation — adding mass or resilient channels to an already-finished wall.

finding: Automatic transfer switch ATS-2 is specified as a closed-transition type. The generator specification requires open-transition transfer for warranty compliance. The two specifications conflict.
howItGotThere: Generator and ATS were specified by different engineers. Transfer type was not coordinated between the two specifications.
consequence: Post-award, contractor submits RFI. One specification must govern. If closed-transition is required, the generator warranty is voided unless a different generator is procured.

finding: Domestic water service is sized for a 3" main per the plumbing drawings. Basis of Design documents peak demand calculations that require a 4" service.
howItGotThere: BOD calculations were done in SD. Drawings were produced in DD. The pipe sizing was not revised when the occupancy count increased.
consequence: System underperforms at peak demand. Utility coordination required to upsize service — this involves street work if the main is in the right-of-way.

finding: Security specification requires 100% camera coverage of all entry points. Camera layout drawings show three entry points with no camera coverage.
howItGotThere: Camera layout was produced by the low-voltage contractor during design-assist. The specification requirement was not used as a checklist during drawing review.
consequence: Owner discovers gaps during commissioning walkthrough. Additional cameras, conduit, and cabling required after ceiling is closed.

finding: Cooling tower specification requires vibration isolation but no vibration isolation specification section exists and no isolator schedule is included in the drawings.
howItGotThere: Vibration isolation requirement was noted in the equipment spec. The isolation system itself was assumed to be included but never explicitly scoped.
consequence: Contractor excludes vibration isolation. Equipment is installed hard-mounted. Noise and vibration complaints from adjacent spaces lead to retrofit.

finding: Basis of Design requires maximum 3% voltage drop to any critical load. Feeder schedule shows conductors sized for 5% voltage drop to the most remote panel.
howItGotThere: Voltage drop calculations were done in SD using a 5% criteria. The BOD was updated to 3% in DD but the feeder schedule was not revised.
consequence: Critical IT equipment operates outside voltage tolerance. Either feeders are resized (conductor replacement in installed conduit) or UPS systems are added.

finding: Project specification includes commissioning requirements in Division 23 and 26. Division 01 contains no commissioning section, no commissioning authority scope, and no reference to the commissioning specification.
howItGotThere: Commissioning was added late in the documentation phase. Division 01 was not updated to reflect the addition.
consequence: General contractor treats commissioning as a subcontractor issue. Coordination between trades during commissioning is not managed at the GC level. Delays compound.

finding: Wall section details show continuous air barrier at the typical condition. At the roof-to-wall interface at the mechanical penthouse, no air barrier detail exists and continuity is not addressed.
howItGotThere: Air barrier was designed for typical conditions. Atypical conditions — the penthouse interface — were not specifically detailed.
consequence: Building fails air leakage testing. Remediation requires opening the wall assembly at the penthouse perimeter.

finding: Utility requires revenue-grade metering at the service entrance. Specification calls for a standard meter socket. No revenue-grade meter, no meter socket enclosure rated for revenue metering, and no utility coordination documentation is included.
howItGotThere: Revenue metering requirement came from the utility company late in the design process. The specification was not updated.
consequence: Utility will not energize the service until revenue-grade metering is installed. Project energization is delayed while the correct equipment is procured.

finding: VAV terminal units are specified as pressure-dependent. The BAS specification calls for pressure-independent control at every terminal unit. The two specifications are incompatible.
howItGotThere: Mechanical and controls specifications were written by different engineers without cross-referencing each other.
consequence: Post-award, contractor submits RFI. Pressure-independent VAV boxes are significantly more expensive. Change order is issued.
